planting.note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in sharply well-drained, gritty or sandy soil in partial shade in fall, 6–10 weeks before first frost; fox's grape fritillary is native to rocky slopes and meadows in Turkey and Iraq and demands excellent drainage — allow soil to dry out through summer dormancy as wet summer conditions will rot the small bulbs. A sunny or lightly shaded site with lean, well-drained soil gives the best results.

directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in very well-drained, gritty, lean soil in partial shade in autumn for April–May bloom; fox's grape fritillary is a charming small fritillary from Turkey and Iraq producing slender stems that carry one to three pendant, bell-shaped flowers with a distinctive and striking bicolor pattern — the outer surface of each bell is a rich purple-brown to chocolate color with golden-yellow tips at the petal ends, while the inner surface is washed in the same warm yellow, creating an elegant contrast reminiscent of a dark grape with a glowing interior. At just 6–10 inches tall, it is one of the smaller fritillaries and is best appreciated at close range in a rock garden, raised bed, or front of a mixed border. Like most Middle Eastern fritillaries, it requires dry summer dormancy and will rot in wet or heavy soils; excellent drainage is essential. It naturalizes slowly in suitable conditions. A refined and underused spring bulb for gardeners seeking alternatives to the common snake's head or crown imperial fritillaries.
sources: The Bulb Garden — Pacific Bulb Society

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in sharply well-drained, gritty or sandy soil in partial shade or sun in fall, 6–10 weeks before first frost; Michael's fritillary is native to alpine and subalpine meadows in northeastern Turkey and must have excellent drainage year-round, particularly dry conditions during summer dormancy — planting in raised beds or incorporating coarse grit into heavy soils is strongly recommended.

directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in sharply well-drained, gritty soil in partial shade or full sun in autumn for April–May bloom; Michael's fritillary is one of the most visually striking of all small fritillaries — a compact, 4–8-inch species from the mountains of northeastern Turkey producing clusters of pendant, bell-shaped flowers whose coloring is among the most dramatic of the genus: the upper two-thirds of each bell is a deep, rich purple-maroon to mahogany-purple, shading abruptly to a bright, clear canary-yellow band at the flared petal tips, creating an intensely eye-catching bicolor effect quite unlike anything else in the spring garden. Multiple bells are carried per stem, intensifying the display. Like all Middle Eastern alpine fritillaries, it insists on perfect drainage and a dry summer dormancy; it is intolerant of prolonged wet conditions at any season and will fail in heavy, moisture-retentive soils. Best grown in a gravel garden, raised bed, alpine trough, or bulb frame. When conditions suit, it multiplies freely by bulb offsets. A rare but readily available gem for the enthusiast collector.
sources: The Bulb Garden — Pacific Bulb Society

planting.note: Plant pips (rhizome divisions) shallowly in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; the crown should rest just at or barely below soil level. Pink lily of the valley spreads by rhizomes like the white species and can be invasive in ideal conditions — plant where it can naturalize freely or contain with buried edging.

directions: Plant pips shallowly in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; pink lily of the valley is the rare pink-flowered cultivar of the beloved woodland groundcover, producing the same pairs of broad, ribbed, deep-green leaves and the same arching sprays of tiny, perfectly formed, intensely fragrant pendant bells as the familiar white species — but in a delicate, warm soft pink that gives a distinctly different and romantic character to the planting. The flowers are sweetly and powerfully fragrant, as in the white form, and bloom in late spring (April–May). It spreads by underground rhizomes to form dense colonies, going summer-dormant as conditions warm. It thrives in cool, moist northern climates and woodland gardens where conditions mimic its native European forest habitat. All parts are highly toxic if ingested. Pips of the pink form may be slightly slower to establish than the white species but are fully as hardy and ultimately just as vigorous. An exceptional and coveted form for the shade garden.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in early spring just as new growth emerges, or plant in early fall, placing rhizomes horizontally 1–2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; variegated Solomon's seal establishes slowly but becomes a long-lived, low-maintenance shade perennial. Mark the planting area as it emerges late in spring.

directions: Plant rhizomes horizontally 1–2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; variegated Solomon's seal is the most widely grown and most ornamentally striking form of Polygonatum odoratum (angular Solomon's seal), bearing the same elegantly arching unbranched stems as the species but with each oval leaf edged in a broad, creamy-white to pale-cream margin that provides striking variegated foliage from emergence in spring through autumn dormancy. In late spring, small pairs of white, sweetly fragrant, tubular pendant bells dangle beneath the leaf axils along the full length of the arching stem, followed by blue-black berries. The arching stems with their precisely alternating variegated leaves create one of the most refined and architecturally graceful effects available for the shaded garden, combining beautifully with hostas, ferns, hellebores, and dark-leaved plants where the creamy leaf margins glow in low light. It spreads slowly by rhizomes into gradually expanding colonies. All parts are toxic if ingested. An indispensable classic of the shade garden.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in early spring or early fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; heartleaf foamflower spreads by stolons to form a dense groundcover — space plants 12–18 inches apart to allow for spreading, and avoid planting too deeply as the crown must remain at soil level.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; heartleaf foamflower is one of the finest native eastern North American woodland wildflowers and shade groundcovers, producing low rosettes of heart-shaped, lobed, dark-green leaves that develop attractive bronze to maroon central markings in autumn and persist through winter in milder climates. In April and May, slender 6–12-inch spikes rise above the foliage covered in masses of tiny, star-shaped, bright-white to pale-pink flowers with prominent stamens that give a soft, feathery, foamy appearance — the source of its evocative common name. It spreads vigorously by stolons (runners) to form a dense, weed-suppressing groundcover carpet under deciduous trees and in shaded borders. Unlike the clump-forming Tiarella wherryi, T. cordifolia is a robust, stoloniferous spreader that naturalizes effectively across large shaded areas. It combines beautifully with wild ginger, native ferns, trilliums, and bleeding heart in woodland garden compositions.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in early spring or early fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Asian foamflower is clump-forming rather than stoloniferous and will not spread aggressively, making it suitable for smaller gardens or planting alongside slower-growing companions where T. cordifolia would be too vigorous.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Asian foamflower is the East Asian counterpart to the native North American foamflowers, native to woodland and montane forest habitats across China, Japan, and the Himalayas, producing a similar but distinctly clump-forming habit rather than the stoloniferous spreading growth of Tiarella cordifolia. It forms neat, compact rosettes of lobed, heart-shaped, softly hairy leaves topped in spring and early summer by slender spikes of tiny, starry, white to pale-pink flowers with the same feathery, foamy appearance that characterizes the genus. At 8–12 inches tall and non-spreading, it is well suited to smaller shade gardens, mixed perennial borders in shade, or planting alongside equally well-behaved companions such as small hostas, epimediums, and hellebores. It is slightly less cold-hardy than T. cordifolia, performing best in zones 5–8. An elegant and underused species that rewards close planting and examination of its delicate spring flowers.
sources: Plants of the World Online — Kew Gardens
